scenario.title: Autonomous Emergency Brake
scenario.narrative: A self-driving car crashed. The brake logic has 4 variables. The Judge warns you: 'Counsel, the court budget is strict. You can only afford to subpoena 3 crash test logs out of the 16 combinations.'
scenario.code:
if obstacle_ahead and speed_gt_50 and not_wet and no_manual_brake:
    trigger_emergency_brake()

claim: I can't test all 16 combinations! Pick the 3 most crucial ones to prove the system fails.
charges: The defense argues that testing every combination (BCC) is physically impossible for large systems.
The court demands you select a subset of 3 critical crash scenarios to subpoena.
Pick any 3 you think are most important.
correct_answer_explanation: Debrief: You subpoenaed 3 logs, but you left 13 combinations completely untested! If this car had 10 variables, there would be 1,024 combinations. BCC is perfect, but physically impossible. There must be a mathematically cheaper way to test (Enter MC/DC in the next module!).
wrong_answer_explanation: You must pick exactly 3 combinations to meet the court budget.
hints: Yargıç Notu: Elimizde sadece 3 celp hakkı var ve tabloda 16 olasılık duruyor.

Bu görevde hangi 3 logu seçtiğinin teknik olarak bir önemi yok. Amaç, kanunun gerektirdiği tüm kombinasyonların sınırlı bütçeyle test edilemeyeceğini mahkemeye kanıtlamak. Rastgele 3 kritik senaryo seç ve geride ne kadar büyük bir boşluk kaldığını izle.

misconceptions.explanation_md: As N grows, BCC becomes practically impossible due to 'combinatorial explosion' (2^N). This paves the way for MC/DC, which guarantees detection of critical interactions with only N+1 tests.
